src-gamberorosso-marino.writer_profile: Valentina Marino is a food journalist and contributor at Gambero Rosso, Italy's leading culinary publication and restaurant guide. Her January 2024 Catania guide surveys ten restaurants selected by independent editorial criteria across price levels and cuisine categories, covering the city's historic seafood tables through contemporary bistrots.
src-gamberorosso-marino.article_title: Dove mangiare a Catania. I 10 migliori ristoranti scelti dal Gambero Rosso

src-finedininglovers-bruno.writer_profile: Mariarosaria Bruno is a named contributor to Fine Dining Lovers Italia, a digital food publication covering restaurant culture and dining internationally. Her September 2024 Catania guide surveys restaurants and bars across the city, with coverage spanning contemporary Sicilian dining and established local institutions.
src-finedininglovers-bruno.article_title: Dove mangiare e bere a Catania: i migliori ristoranti e bar
src-finedininglovers-bruno.commercial_conflict_note: Fine Dining Lovers is published by S.Pellegrino/Sanpellegrino, sponsor of the World's 50 Best Restaurants awards.

src-postcards-spencer.writer_profile: Gayle Brennan Spencer is the sole named author of Postcards From San Antonio, a personal travel and food blog. Named author confirmed via the site's About page (single-author site rule applied — byline on About page only, not on individual article headers). Her May 2025 Catania article covers ten restaurants visited during a stay, with specific dish-level detail and personal assessment.
src-postcards-spencer.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?

src-cultureandterroir-lirosi.writer_profile: Paolo Li Rosi is a named author at Culture and Terroir, an Italian food and wine culture publication. His December 2023 article covers the Slow Food Osterie d'Italia 2024 guide launch in Catania, documenting Me Cumpari Turiddu's receipt of the Chiocciola award and the Selezione Bere Bene prize.
src-cultureandterroir-lirosi.article_title: OSTERIE D'ITALIA 2024, Slow Food fa tappa a Catania

restaurants.name: Me Cumpari Turiddu
dishes_mentioned: poached egg on toasted bread with fried leeks
crispella with anchovies and orange aioli
restaurants.quote: Named after emigrant memories and grandmother's kitchen expressions, this bistrot features deeply rooted and identity-driven Sicilian dishes, from home-made breads to creative appetizers and poached egg on toasted bread with fried leeks.
restaurants.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?

restaurants.name: Vuciata Kitchen Market
restaurants.quote: Located in Catania's fish market and operated by two pairs of brothers, the kitchen serves as a hymn to the soul and market products, with marinated swordfish carpaccio, daily mixed fry, and bottarga spaghetti.
restaurants.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?

restaurants.name: Fud Bottega Sicula
restaurants.quote: Visited twice — blue cheese burgers, buffalo burgers, and vegetable variations, plus a gorgonzola and pear salad that speaks well of Sicilian quality ingredients in a burger format.
restaurants.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?

restaurants.name: Amocù
restaurants.quote: Tradition guides the Japanese line but the menu circles around the world — quality base products, comprehensive wine list, and attentive service make this a distinctive dining destination in Ognina.
restaurants.article_title: Dove mangiare a Catania. I 10 migliori ristoranti scelti dal Gambero Rosso

restaurants.name: Osteria Antica Marina
restaurants.quote: Located in the heart of the Pescheria fish market, this iconic venue serves solid historic dishes tied to local sourcing, with an impressive antipasti spread in a recently renovated space with Sicilian wines.
restaurants.article_title: Dove mangiare a Catania. I 10 migliori ristoranti scelti dal Gambero Rosso

restaurants.name: Materia Spazio Cucina
name_variants: Materia Spazio Cucina di Habitat Boutique Hotel
restaurants.quote: A hospitality project featuring a journey among small Sicilian excellences, with breakfast using local products, short lunch menus, and full gourmet dinners organized by ingredient — fish market, butchery, vegetables, desserts.
restaurants.article_title: Dove mangiare a Catania. I 10 migliori ristoranti scelti dal Gambero Rosso

restaurants.name: Pamochã
restaurants.quote: An acronym for Bread, Mortadella and Champagne, this young welcoming venue operates as a champagne bar serving oysters, tartares, duck breast, and Irish beef with outdoor seating and skilled service.
restaurants.article_title: Dove mangiare a Catania. I 10 migliori ristoranti scelti dal Gambero Rosso

restaurants.name: Le Tre Bocche
restaurants.quote: A steadfast seafood table with first-quality catch and engaging staff, featuring extensive antipasti service with raw and cooked preparations, thoughtfully selected wines, and a classical comfortable ambiance.
restaurants.article_title: Dove mangiare a Catania. I 10 migliori ristoranti scelti dal Gambero Rosso

restaurants.name: Uzeta Bistrò Siciliano
restaurants.quote: In constant evolution, this contemporary bistrot serves as a laboratory with international profile, where chef Francesco Di Stefano experiments with reinterpreted street food classics like arancino with nonna's spiced sauce, panelle chips, tartares, and seafood preparations.
restaurants.article_title: Dove mangiare a Catania. I 10 migliori ristoranti scelti dal Gambero Rosso

restaurants.name: Concezione Restaurant
restaurants.quote: Under chef Manuel Tropea's direction, this minimalist space focuses on creative Sicilian cuisine with new visions on local tradition and Sicilian recipes, reinterpreting the island's gastronomic heritage with a contemporary eye.
restaurants.article_title: Dove mangiare e bere a Catania: i migliori ristoranti e bar

restaurants.name: Bat il Trattore
restaurants.quote: A family-run establishment in the Monserrato district featuring sardines beneficcata, insalata nonna with truly vine-ripened tomatoes, and pasta alla Norma — warmly traditional Sicilian home cooking.
restaurants.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?

restaurants.name: Bavetta l'Osteria d'Oggi
dishes_mentioned: squash blossoms with ricotta and anchovies
pasta with lemon mint pesto and tuna bottarga
restaurants.quote: Chef Marco Cannizzaro's contemporary neighborhood tavern, requiring reservations, serves squash blossoms with ricotta and anchovies, roasted onions with mint, and pasta with lemon mint pesto and tuna bottarga.
restaurants.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?

restaurants.name: Osteria Acqualavica
restaurants.quote: Upscale wine cellar atmosphere with stone-arched ceilings offering eggplant polpette, seared tuna with mint, and an ultra-lemony tuna risotto.
restaurants.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?

restaurants.name: Scirocco Sicilian Fish Lab
restaurants.quote: Casual spot near the fish market offering seafood couscous and fried seafood cones at budget prices — elbow your way in to partake of the house specialties.
restaurants.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?

restaurants.name: Tantikkia Cucina e Vino
dishes_mentioned: sesame-crusted tuna in green tea sauce
restaurants.signature_dish: sesame-crusted tuna in green tea sauce
restaurants.quote: A handsome interior feeling like a comforting cocoon, featuring Tuscan kale sides, sesame-crusted tuna in green tea sauce, and pasta with anchovies.
restaurants.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?

restaurants.name: La Pentolaccia
restaurants.quote: A Catania trattoria in the historic centre featuring fennel and orange salad and seafood risotto — reliable neighbourhood cooking with consistent quality.
restaurants.article_title: Postcard from Catania, Sicily: Where's the cannoli?
